one_line_takeaway: The paper gives an exact information-theoretic capacity characterization for covert multi-bit LLM watermarking under a block-autoregressive (multi-token-prediction-inspired) model, and an explicit CMDP + polar-code algorithm that achieves under 10% BER at 0.375 bits/token on LLaMA3.2 1B with negligible perplexity cost, though extension beyond short blocks (L=8) is explicitly left as future work due to exponential state-space growth.
key_finding: Empirically, on LLaMA3.2 1B (temperature 0.8, top-K=50 sampling, Q=20 retained candidate sequences per block, prompts drawn from the C4 News dataset, block length L=8, B=2 blocks, covertness constraint epsilon=0.1, discount gamma=1), the scheme achieves a bit-error rate below 10% at an embedding rate of 0.375 bits/token, with perplexity remaining largely invariant relative to the base model across the tested rate range (perplexity roughly 1.40-1.50, watermarked vs. base curves close together) -- i.e., the paper's stated headline result from the abstract is reproduced quantitatively in Fig. 2 (top). Fig. 2 (bottom) shows that increasing the number of independent keys from 1 to 8 reduces average total variation (roughly from ~0.10 down toward ~0.04-0.06 depending on rate), consistent with soft-covering theory; TV is minimized near the theoretically required key-rate threshold of ceil(I(U^(b);X^(b)|S^(b))) = 3 bits under this setup, but for finite block length L=8 the polarization is insufficient to fully realize the average-TV constraint epsilon/L, so higher message rates force use of state-constrained code positions and TV begins to increase again once key rate exceeds 3 bits.
core_contribution: The paper develops an information-theoretic framework for covert multi-bit LLM watermarking. It models watermark embedding as a block-autoregressive process inspired by multi-token-prediction LLM architectures, in which the encoder is given limited non-causal access to the token distribution within a block (rather than strict left-to-right causal access, as in prior autoregressive formulations). Under this model the authors (1) derive an exact capacity characterization (Theorem 1) for covert multi-bit watermarking by combining Gelfand-Pinsker (GP) coding with channel-resolvability/synthesis coding, expressed as max_{p(u|s),W(x|u,s)} I(U;X) - I(U;S) subject to the marginal-consistency constraint, achievable at key rate R_k = I(U;X|S); (2) give a closed-form binary-alphabet example (Corollary 2) showing an achievable rate of up to 0.5 bits/token as vocabulary size V grows, obtained by partitioning the vocabulary into two balanced subsets and encoding via subset membership; (3) formulate the multi-block embedding-distribution optimization as a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP), with reward I(U^(b);X^(b)) - I(U^(b);S^(b)) and cost the total-variation covertness penalty, solved via a primal-dual policy-gradient algorithm (Algorithm 1); and (4) construct an explicit polar-coding scheme (Section IV-B) that realizes the GP/resolvability scheme at finite block length, including a modified successive-cancellation encoding/decoding rule (Eq. 17-18) to handle the non-i.i.d. dependence introduced by channel memory across positions in a block, plus a one-time-pad (OTP) step on the first ceil(I(U^(b);X^(b)|S^(b))) message bits per block for covertness.
threat_model: This is not an attacker-vs-defender security paper in the classical sense; the 'adversary' is a passive observer who has access to the watermarked text but not the secret key K or the embedded message M. The security/covertness requirement is that the induced token distribution under any message m, averaged over keys, be statistically close (vanishing total variation) to the base (unwatermarked) LLM output distribution, so such an observer cannot detect that embedding occurred, distinguish messages, or recover the message without the key (joint covertness + semantic secrecy, Eq. 8). Reliability is measured as decoding error probability for an authorized party holding the key. The scheme assumes the decoder has access to the induced joint distribution P_{S^(b)U^(b)X^(b)} learned by the encoder (Remark 2) -- an intermediate regime between full white-box model access and pure black-box decoding, not the standard fully black-box LLM-watermark-detector assumption.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation (Remark 3) is that the scheme is computationally difficult to extend to longer token blocks because the state space of S^(b) grows exponentially across blocks, making both the CMDP encoding-distribution optimization and the Bhattacharyya-parameter computation for polar-code construction (Eq. 15) increasingly intractable as block length increases; they suggest scalable approximations such as mismatched decoding or neural alternatives to Algorithm 1 as future work. Additional caveats visible in the text itself: (i) the decoder is assumed to know the encoder's induced joint distribution P_{S^(b)U^(b)X^(b)} per block (Remark 2), a non-standard, non-fully-black-box assumption; (ii) the multi-token, non-causal-access abstraction requires replacing standard post-sampling verification/filtering in multi-token-prediction decoding with a pre-sampling reweighting scheme (footnote 1), which is an approximation of how real multi-token-prediction LLMs (e.g., DeepSeek-V3-style, speculative decoding) actually operate; (iii) evaluation is on a single small model (LLaMA3.2 1B) with a single small block configuration (L=8, B=2) and a single prompt source (C4 News); no comparison experiments against other multi-bit watermarking baselines (e.g., the codable/error-control-coding schemes cited as related work [6]-[9], or the causal-side-information capacity result [10]) are run empirically -- comparisons to prior work are made only at the level of capacity formulas/regimes (e.g., Corollary 2 vs. the causal scheme of [10]), not head-to-head experiments.

datasets_used: C4 News (prompt source only, used to define the operational capacity/state distribution)
quantitative_results: Bit-error rate (BER) below 10% at embedding rate 0.375 bits/token, with negligible perplexity/distortion degradation
Watermarked-vs-base perplexity approximately 1.40-1.50 across tested embedding rates, remaining largely invariant to rate
Achievable rate limit in Corollary 2 (binary-token-pair example): R -> 0.5 bits/token as vocabulary size V -> infinity, with key rate R_k equal to the message rate

Theoretically required key rate for covertness in the empirical setup: ceil(I(U^(b);X^(b)|S^(b))) = 3 bits; average total variation is minimized near this threshold and increases again once key rate exceeds it
Average total variation decreases (roughly from ~0.10 toward ~0.04-0.06, rate-dependent) as the number of keys used increases from 1 to 8
